content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

May 22, 1997

Randy Groom
Second Vice President
City Clerks Association of California
City of Visalia
Post Office Box 5078
Visalia, California 93278

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-97-060

Dear Mr. Groom:

In your position as the Second Vice President of the City Clerks Association of California ("CCAC"), you have requested advice on behalf of the CCAC regarding Section 85705, a new provision added by Proposition 208 to the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ We are rendering informal assistance since you are not requesting advice on behalf of a specific individual or regarding a specific governmental decision.²

QUESTION

Does Section 85705 and the term "public board or commission" apply to members of an advisory committee appointed by the city council?

CONCLUSION

Section 85705 applies to members of an appointed advisory committee which is a governmental entity, with the authority to make governmental decisions and whose members are public officials.

_______________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329, subd. (c)(3).)

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. I-97-060
Page 2

FACTS

Many California cities utilize advisory committees comprised of citizens appointed by the
city council. Such committees are generally organized and its members are appointed for a
specific purpose. In many situations, the members of the advisory committees do not serve
fixed terms, do not make final decisions and do not file a statement of economic interests. In
addition, they have neither city staff support nor funding.

ANALYSIS

Section 85705, which was added by Proposition 208, states as follows:

"No person appointed to a public board or commission or as
Trustee of the California State University or Regent of the
University of California during tenure in office shall donate to, or
solicit or accept any campaign contribution for, any committee
controlled by the person who made the appointment to that office
or any other entity with the intent that the recipient of the donation
be any committee controlled by such person who made the
appointment."

For the prohibitions of this section to apply, a person has to be appointed to a public
board or commission. However, the above statute neither defines nor gives guidance as to what
in fact is a public board or commission. In addition, the term "public board or commission" is
not defined in the Act.

Therefore, as we have interpreted this section, a public board or commission includes a
local advisory committee which is a governmental entity under Section 82041, has the authority
to make governmental decisions and whose members are public officials. (Lyons Advice Letter,
No. A-97-203, Regulation 18700(a)(1), copy enclosed, gives the criteria for decisionmaking
authority. (See also Moll Advice Letter, No. A-97-161, where the members appointed to a
board which is created pursuant to the city charter; Holland Advice Letter, No. A-97-120, where
the Office of the Attorney General determined that Private Industry Councils are governmental
entities under Section 82041 and the members are public officials; and the Abdelnour Advice
Letter, No. A-97-164, and Vasquez-Connolly Advice Letter, No. A-97-181, which discuss
appointments, copies enclosed.)

File No. I-97-060
Page 3

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Jill Stecher
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:JS:ak

Enclosures
